messages.content: A pipe burst in my basement last night and everything is flooded, how do I file a claim?

For a house resident: To file a claim for the pipe burst and subsequent flooding in your basement, follow these steps:

1. **Contact the claims department**: Reach out to your insurance company's claims department as soon as possible. You can do this through the policyholder portal, the mobile app, or by phone. Have your policy number ready to provide to the representative.
2. **Provide a description of the loss**: When you contact the claims department, be prepared to provide a description of what happened, including the date, time, and circumstances of the loss. In this case, you can explain that a pipe burst in your basement, causing flooding.
3. **Take photos and document the damage**: Before any cleanup or repair work begins, take photos of the damage to your basement and any affected belongings. This will help support your claim and provide evidence of the extent of the damage.
4. **Keep receipts for emergency repairs**: If you need to make any emergency repairs to prevent further damage, such as hiring a water extraction service, keep receipts for these expenses. These may be reimbursable as part of your claim.
5. **Wait for an adjuster to be assigned**: After you file your claim, an adjuster will be assigned to inspect the damage and estimate the cost of repair or replacement. They will guide you through the next steps in the process.
6. **Receive a claim number**: Once your claim is filed, you will receive a claim number, which you can use to track the status of your claim.
7. **Complete a proof of loss form**: Your adjuster will provide guidance on completing a proof of loss form, which itemizes damaged or destroyed property and its estimated value. This form is typically required within 60 days of the loss, so be sure to complete it accurately and on time.

Remember to carefully follow the instructions provided by your insurance company and adjuster to ensure that your claim is processed efficiently and effectively. If you have any questions or concerns, don't hesitate to reach out to your adjuster or insurance company for guidance.

retrieved_context:
Section 5: Filing a Claim
To file a claim, contact the claims department as soon as possible after a loss, either through the
policyholder portal, the mobile app, or by phone, and provide your policy number and a description of
what happened, including the date, time, and circumstances of the loss as best as can be determined.
An adjuster will be assigned to inspect the damage and estimate the cost of repair or replacement.
Keep receipts for any emergency repairs made to prevent further damage, as these may be
reimbursable, and take photos of the damage before any cleanup or repair work begins whenever it is
safe to do so.
Once a claim is filed, you will receive a claim number for tracking. Active claims should be handled with

careful, step-by-step guidance, since documentation requirements and deadlines directly affect the
outcome and payout of the claim, and missing a required document deadline can delay or reduce your
settlement.
For water, fire, or storm damage claims, most policies require you to take reasonable steps to prevent
further damage to the property, such as tarping a damaged roof or extracting standing water, and the
reasonable cost of these protective measures is generally reimbursable as part of the claim.
A proof of loss form, itemizing damaged or destroyed property and its estimated value, is typically
required within 60 days of the loss for the claim to proceed to settlement; your adjuster can provide
guidance on completing this form accurately and on time.

Business activities conducted from the home may require a separate business owner's policy, as this
policy's liability and property coverage is intended for personal, non-commercial use, and claims arising
from business equipment, inventory, or client visits are typically denied under a standard homeowners
policy.
Damage from backed-up sewers or drains is excluded unless a water backup endorsement has been
added to the policy, which is a common and relatively inexpensive add-on that many homeowners
choose given how frequently this type of loss occurs compared to some named perils.
Certain dog breeds and exotic pets may be excluded from liability coverage depending on state
regulations and insurer underwriting guidelines; check your declarations page or contact your agent if

or endorsements purchased individually; flood coverage is typically obtained through the National Flood
Insurance Program or a private flood carrier, while earthquake coverage is available as a separate
endorsement or standalone policy depending on region.
Replacement cost coverage, when selected, pays to rebuild or repair damage without a deduction for
depreciation, whereas actual cash value coverage deducts depreciation based on the age and
condition of the damaged structure, resulting in a lower payout for older homes.
An ordinance or law endorsement can be added to cover the increased cost of rebuilding to meet
